one_line_takeaway: RobustKD reduces a backdoored teacher's feature variance via an optimized detoxification mask before feature distillation, cutting the compressed student's backdoor ASR to under 10% across four datasets at a ~3-4% accuracy cost.
key_finding: Across four datasets and four teacher/student pairs, RobustKD reduces the student's attack success rate (ASR) to below 10% in every case while incurring only ~3-4% accuracy loss relative to standard FKD. The paper's headline claim (Introduction) is that RobustKD reduces ASR by an average of 85%, which it states is 75% higher than SOTA baselines, and that the distilled student achieves at least an 80% (up to 90%) backdoor removal rate versus FKD. Example ASR drops (Table 4): CIFAR-100 WRN28-4->ResNet56 under LBA falls from FKD's 72.84% to 0.72%, and under CKD from 93.58% to 3.31%; ImageNet Pyramid-200->WRN28-4 under CKD falls from FKD's 53.34% to 6.93%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes RobustKD, a robust feature-based knowledge distillation (FKD) method that simultaneously compresses a large teacher DNN into a lightweight student AND mitigates a backdoor that would otherwise transfer from a backdoored teacher to the student during distillation. The authors frame this as the first work to jointly target robust distillation against backdoor attacks and model compression, arguing that prior work either compresses without robustness (standard KD/FKD) or removes backdoors without producing a compressed student (e.g., NAD). The technical mechanism rests on an empirical observation of the authors' own (Fig. 2): the activation feature variance of a backdoored model is significantly larger than that of a clean model. RobustKD 'detoxifies' the teacher's features by optimizing a mask that minimizes this feature variance, then distills the detoxified features into the student while a cross-entropy term preserves clean-task accuracy. Code is released at github.com/Xming-Z/RobustKD.
threat_model: Model supply-chain attack in a compression/deployment setting with three parties: model supplier, model deployer, and end user. The attacker is a malicious model supplier who trains a teacher DNN with full control of architecture, parameters, and training data, implants a backdoor, and publishes the poisoned teacher to a public repository (the paper demonstrates this concretely by uploading a WRN28-4 model poisoned with LBA on CIFAR-100 to Hugging Face, model ID 30e411e). The defender is the model deployer, who downloads the untrusted teacher, cannot access the training process or training data, has only a small set of clean images, and distills the teacher into a smaller student for resource-constrained edge deployment. The threat is that the backdoor survives distillation into the student; the defense goal is to accomplish the compression main task while removing the backdoor from the student.
limitations: (1) The defense costs ~3-4% clean accuracy versus FKD (Table 5), which the authors attribute to detoxification being a black-box process that cannot precisely separate toxic from beneficial feature components. (2) Effectiveness degrades when teacher and student architectures are similar: in that setting the student retains more of the teacher's 'dark knowledge' and RobustKD leaves >10% residual backdoor (Table 6: WRN28-4->WRN16-4 residual ASR 13.32% under CKD). (3) The whole method rests on a single empirical heuristic (backdoored models have higher feature variance) demonstrated on only three attacks on CIFAR-100, not a proven property. (4) Evaluation is limited to backdoor attacks that actually survive distillation: of CKD, LBA, and HTBA, only CKD and LBA transfer (HTBA collapses to ~6-23% ASR after distillation per Table 3), so the defense is only tested against two attacks. (5) Detoxification/Lf is applied only to the last distillation layer. (6) The adaptive-attack results (RQ5) and conclusions fall beyond the extracted page window, so the strength of the adaptive-attack evaluation could not be verified from the retrieved text.

quantitative_results: RobustKD reduces student ASR to <10% in all tested cases across 4 datasets and 4 model pairs (Table 4)
CIFAR-100 WRN28-4->ResNet56: LBA ASR 72.84% (FKD) -> 0.72% (RobustKD); CKD ASR 93.58% -> 3.31%
Paper's headline claim: average 85% ASR reduction, stated as 75% higher than SOTA baselines; >=80% (up to 90%) backdoor removal vs FKD
Clean accuracy cost ~3-4% vs FKD (e.g., CIFAR-100 WRN28-4->WRN16-2 CKD: 78.23% FKD -> 74.57% RobustKD, Table 5)
Similar-architecture case leaves residual backdoor: WRN28-4->WRN16-4 CKD residual ASR 13.32%, WRN28-4->WRN28-2 CKD 10.94% (Table 6)
Hyperparameters: mask LR 0.001, 40 mask rounds, default m=0.2 (optimal near m=0.1), 300 distillation cycles
baselines_compared: FKD (feature knowledge distillation, ref [22]) - primary distillation baseline for both ASR and accuracy
CKD (cheatKD) - transitive backdoor attack used as one of two attacks RobustKD defends against
LBA (latent backdoor attack, ref [33]) - second backdoor attack RobustKD defends against
HTBA (hidden trigger backdoor attack, ref [46]) - tested but does not survive distillation, so not used as a defense target
Referenced defense NAD (neural attention distillation) is discussed as prior work that removes backdoors but does not compress
